events.title: Legendary Founding by Emperor Jimmu
events.detail: According to the Nihon Shoki and Kojiki chronicles, Emperor Jimmu — a descendant of the sun goddess Amaterasu — unified the Yamato region and established the Japanese imperial line on February 11, 660 BCE. This legendary date is commemorated as National Foundation Day (Kenkoku Kinen no Hi). Modern historians regard the early emperors as legendary or semi-legendary, with Japan's verifiable state formation emerging later in the Yamato period.

events.title: Yayoi Period: Wet-Rice Agriculture Transforms Japan
events.detail: The Yayoi period (c. 300 BCE – 300 CE) saw the introduction of wet-rice cultivation, bronze and iron tools, and weaving from the Asian continent via Korea. These innovations drove Japan's transition from the Jōmon hunter-gatherer society to an agricultural civilization. Population grew rapidly, and social hierarchies and proto-states began to emerge in western Japan.

events.title: Yamato State Emerges in Central Japan
events.detail: The Yamato polity — ancestor of the imperial state — emerged in the Kinai region (modern Nara and Osaka) during the Kofun period (c. 250–538 CE). Yamato rulers consolidated power through alliances with regional clans (uji) and constructed massive keyhole-shaped burial mounds (kofun). The Yamato court established diplomatic relations with the Korean kingdoms of Baekje, Goguryeo, and Silla, and with China's various dynasties.

events.title: Buddhism Introduced to Japan from Baekje
events.detail: Buddhism was officially introduced to Japan in 552 CE (some scholars date it to 538 CE) when King Seong of the Korean kingdom of Baekje sent a mission bearing Buddhist sutras and a statue to the Yamato court. The new religion triggered a fierce political struggle between the Soga clan (pro-Buddhism) and the Mononobe clan (anti-Buddhism). The Soga eventually prevailed, and Buddhism became a state religion.

sources.name: Nihon Shoki (Chronicles of Japan, 720 CE) via National Diet Library
UNESCO — Buddhist Monuments in the Horyu-ji Area

events.title: Prince Shōtoku's Seventeen-Article Constitution
events.detail: Prince Shōtoku Taishi promulgated Japan's first written code of governance — 17 moral and administrative articles drawing on Confucian and Buddhist principles. It declared the emperor supreme, required officials to act morally, and established that 'harmony is to be valued.' Shōtoku also sent embassies to China's Sui Dynasty to absorb Chinese political culture and administration, accelerating Japan's state formation.

events.title: Taika Reform Establishes Centralized Imperial State
events.detail: Following the Taika coup in which the Fujiwara and imperial clan eliminated the dominant Soga family, reformers proclaimed the Taika ('Great Change') reform edicts in 646 CE. Modeled on Tang China's bureaucratic system, the reforms abolished private land ownership, established central government ministries, created a national tax system, and mandated a census. These reforms laid the foundation for the Ritsuryō legal code and the Nara state.

sources.name: National Archives of Japan — Taika Reform

events.title: Japan's First Permanent Capital Established at Nara
events.detail: Emperor Genmei moved Japan's capital to Heijō-kyō (modern Nara) in 710 CE, modeled on China's Tang capital Chang'an. The Nara period (710–794) saw the compilation of Japan's two oldest chronicles (Kojiki 712, Nihon Shoki 720) and rapid Buddhist institution-building, including Tōdai-ji temple. The state sponsored Chinese-style governance, art, and scholarship.

sources.name: UNESCO — Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara

events.title: Great Buddha of Nara (Daibutsu) Dedicated
events.detail: Emperor Shōmu commissioned the colossal bronze Great Buddha (Vairocana Buddha) at Tōdai-ji temple, completed and consecrated in 752 CE. The statue stands 15 meters tall and required vast national resources — and artisans from across Asia. Its construction reflected the Nara state's aspiration to use Buddhism as an ideological unifier. Tōdai-ji's Great Hall remains the world's largest wooden structure.

events.title: Capital Moved to Heian-kyō (Kyoto)
events.detail: Emperor Kanmu relocated Japan's capital to Heian-kyō ('Capital of Peace and Tranquility', modern Kyoto) in 794 CE, partly to escape the political influence of Nara's powerful Buddhist temples. Kyoto would serve as Japan's imperial capital for over 1,000 years until 1869. The Heian period (794–1185) saw the flourishing of native Japanese culture — kana writing, waka poetry, mono no aware aesthetics, and court romance literature.

sources.name: UNESCO — Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto

events.title: Fujiwara Clan Establishes Regent System
events.detail: Fujiwara no Yoshifusa became the first non-imperial regent (sesshō) in 858 CE, serving for an emperor who was his grandson. The Fujiwara clan perfected the strategy of marrying daughters into the imperial family, then ruling as regents. Under Fujiwara no Michinaga (966–1028 CE), the clan achieved its peak power — he was father of four empresses. The regency system reduced emperors to figureheads, a pattern that would recur throughout Japanese history.

events.title: Murasaki Shikibu Completes The Tale of Genji
events.detail: Lady Murasaki Shikibu, a court lady serving Empress Shōshi, completed Genji Monogatari (The Tale of Genji) around 1010 CE. Widely considered the world's first novel, it follows the romantic and political life of Hikaru Genji at the Heian court. Murasaki Shikibu's work is Japan's greatest literary achievement and a window into aristocratic Heian culture. It has been translated into more than 20 languages.

sources.name: Encyclopaedia Britannica — The Tale of Genji

events.title: Minamoto no Yoritomo Establishes Japan's First Shogunate
events.detail: After defeating the rival Taira clan in the Genpei War, Minamoto no Yoritomo established the Kamakura shogunate in 1185 (formally granted shogunal title in 1192). This military government (bakufu) in Kamakura ruled alongside the imperial court in Kyoto. Power shifted decisively to the warrior class (samurai/bushi). The shogunate model of military-dominated governance would persist — with interruptions — until 1868.

sources.name: National Archives of Japan — Kamakura Period

events.title: First Mongol Invasion of Japan Repelled
events.detail: Kublai Khan's Yuan dynasty fleet of 900 ships and 40,000 troops landed at Hakata Bay (Fukuoka) in November 1274. Japanese samurai, unaccustomed to Mongol formation tactics and exploding bombs, suffered heavy casualties. However, a violent storm destroyed much of the Mongol fleet, forcing withdrawal. The Japanese interpreted the storms as divine intervention — the origin of the term 'kamikaze' (divine wind).

sources.name: Encyclopaedia Britannica — Mongol Invasions of Japan

events.title: Second Mongol Invasion Destroyed by Typhoon (Kamikaze)
events.detail: Kublai Khan launched a second, massive invasion with 140,000 troops aboard 4,400 ships — possibly the largest amphibious operation in medieval history. The Japanese held defensive walls at Hakata Bay while a catastrophic typhoon (kamikaze, 'divine wind') struck in August 1281, destroying approximately 4,000 Mongol ships and killing tens of thousands of soldiers. Japan was never again invaded until 1945.

sources.name: National Archives of Japan — Mongol Invasions

events.title: Ashikaga Takauji Establishes Muromachi Shogunate
events.detail: After betraying Emperor Go-Daigo's Kenmu Restoration, Ashikaga Takauji established a new shogunate in Kyoto (Muromachi district) in 1336. The Muromachi period (1336–1573) saw the rise of Zen Buddhism, tea ceremony, Noh theater, ink painting, and the iconic Japanese garden aesthetic. It also saw Japan's greatest civil strife — the Southern and Northern Courts schism (1336–1392) and the devastating Ōnin War.

events.title: Ōnin War Triggers the Sengoku (Warring States) Period
events.detail: The Ōnin War (1467–1477) erupted from a succession dispute within the Ashikaga shogunate and spread into a brutal decade-long conflict that laid waste to Kyoto. The war shattered central authority and triggered the Sengoku ('Warring States') period, in which powerful regional lords (daimyo) competed for dominance. Over 130 years of civil war followed before Japan was reunified in the late 16th century.

sources.name: National Archives of Japan — Sengoku Period

events.title: Portuguese Traders Arrive, Introducing Firearms to Japan
events.detail: Portuguese traders shipwrecked on Tanegashima island in 1543, introducing firearms (arquebuses) to Japan. Local daimyo quickly recognized the military potential and commissioned Japanese smiths to copy European guns. Within decades, firearms revolutionized Japanese warfare. Christian missionaries — including Francis Xavier, who arrived in 1549 — also came with Portuguese traders, converting an estimated 300,000 Japanese before persecution began.

sources.name: National Archives of Japan — Portugal Relations

events.title: Oda Nobunaga Enters Kyoto, Begins National Unification
events.detail: Oda Nobunaga marched into Kyoto in 1568, ending the Ashikaga shogunate's political relevance. A visionary and ruthless innovator, Nobunaga pioneered combined-arms tactics using ashigaru (foot soldiers) with firearms en masse at the Battle of Nagashino (1575), crushing cavalry-based armies. He was assassinated by his general Akechi Mitsuhide at Honnō-ji temple in Kyoto in 1582 before completing unification.

events.title: Toyotomi Hideyoshi Completes Japan's Unification
events.detail: Born a peasant's son, Toyotomi Hideyoshi rose to become Nobunaga's top general and successor. By 1590, he completed Japan's unification by besieging Odawara Castle (the Hōjō stronghold). Hideyoshi launched two failed invasions of Korea (1592–93, 1597–98), seeking to conquer China. He died in 1598 before returning from Korea. His 'sword hunt' (1588) disarmed the peasantry, cementing samurai dominance.

sources.name: National Archives of Japan — Toyotomi Period

events.title: Battle of Sekigahara: Tokugawa Victory Decides Japan's Future
events.detail: On October 21, 1600, Tokugawa Ieyasu's eastern coalition defeated Ishida Mitsunari's western coalition in a six-hour battle at Sekigahara (Gifu Prefecture). With approximately 160,000 combatants, it was arguably the largest battle in Japanese history. Ieyasu's victory effectively ended the Warring States period and paved the way for 265 years of Tokugawa rule. The battle site remains a national historic landmark.

sources.name: National Archives of Japan — Battle of Sekigahara

events.title: Tokugawa Ieyasu Establishes Edo Shogunate
events.detail: Tokugawa Ieyasu received the title of shōgun from Emperor Go-Yōzei in 1603, establishing the Edo shogunate (bakufu) headquartered in Edo (modern Tokyo). To prevent future power struggles, he passed the title to his son Hidetada in 1605, establishing hereditary shogunal succession within the Tokugawa family. The shogunate implemented strict controls on daimyo — alternating residence (sankin-kōtai), marriage restrictions, and hostage-holding — ensuring 265 years of peace (Pax Tokugawa).

sources.name: National Archives of Japan — Edo Period

events.title: Sakoku Policy: Japan Closes Borders to Most Foreigners
events.detail: In 1639, the Tokugawa shogunate issued edicts completing its sakoku ('locked country') isolation policy, expelling Portuguese traders and banning most foreign contact. Christianity was banned and persecuted. Only Dutch and Chinese merchants were permitted limited trade at Dejima (an artificial island in Nagasaki harbor). This isolation preserved social stability and reduced foreign military threat but arrested technological modernization — a debt Japan would urgently pay after 1853.

events.title: Commodore Perry's Black Ships Force Japan's Opening
events.detail: US Navy Commodore Matthew C. Perry sailed four steam-powered warships ('Black Ships') into Edo Bay on July 8, 1853, demanding trade access on behalf of President Fillmore. The shogunate, unable to resist modern naval power, signed the Convention of Kanagawa on March 31, 1854, opening ports at Shimoda and Hakodate. Perry's arrival traumatically revealed Japan's military vulnerability and precipitated the political crisis that ended the shogunate.

sources.name: National Archives of Japan — Perry Expedition

events.title: Meiji Restoration: Emperor Regains Power, Ending 265-Year Shogunate
events.detail: In January 1868, pro-imperial forces defeated Tokugawa loyalists (Boshin War) and restored imperial rule under the 15-year-old Emperor Meiji. The new government moved the capital from Kyoto to Edo (renamed Tokyo). The Charter Oath promised 'knowledge shall be sought throughout the world.' The Meiji Restoration launched the most radical national transformation in Japanese history — from feudal isolation to industrialized empire within 30 years.

sources.name: National Archives of Japan — Meiji Restoration

events.title: Meiji Constitution Proclaimed: Japan's First Written Constitution
events.detail: On February 11, 1889, Emperor Meiji proclaimed Japan's first constitution — drafted primarily by Itō Hirobumi after studying European (especially Prussian) models. The constitution established a bicameral Diet (parliament) but reserved sovereign power to the Emperor, who commanded the military independently of civilian government. This 'separation of civil and military authority' would prove fatally destabilizing under Showa-era militarism.

sources.name: National Archives of Japan — Meiji Constitution

events.title: First Sino-Japanese War: Japan Defeats Qing China, Gains Taiwan
events.detail: Japan's modern armed forces — built in only 27 years — decisively defeated Qing China in the First Sino-Japanese War (August 1894 – April 1895), fought over Korea. The Treaty of Shimonoseki (April 17, 1895) awarded Japan Taiwan, the Pescadores, and the Liaodong Peninsula (returned under the Triple Intervention by Russia, Germany, and France), plus a massive war indemnity. The victory announced Japan as Asia's dominant military power.

sources.name: National Archives of Japan — Sino-Japanese War

events.title: Russo-Japanese War: Japan Defeats Russia in Landmark Victory
events.detail: Japan's stunning defeat of Imperial Russia in the Russo-Japanese War (February 1904 – September 1905) shocked the world — it was the first modern victory by an Asian nation over a European great power. Japan sank Russia's Baltic Fleet at the Battle of Tsushima (May 1905) and captured Port Arthur. The Treaty of Portsmouth (mediated by US President Theodore Roosevelt) gave Japan control over Korea and southern Manchuria, cementing its status as a great power.

events.title: Japan Formally Annexes Korea
events.detail: Following decades of political interference and the 1905 protectorate treaty, Japan formally annexed Korea on August 22, 1910, via the Japan-Korea Annexation Treaty. Korea was renamed 'Chōsen' and governed as a colony until 1945. Colonial policy included suppression of the Korean language and names, forced labor mobilization, and the comfort women system. Japan's 35-year colonial rule remains the central source of Korea-Japan historical tensions.

sources.name: National Archives of Japan — Korea Annexation
United Nations — OHCHR on Historical Records

events.title: Great Kantō Earthquake Devastates Tokyo Region
events.detail: A magnitude 7.9 earthquake struck the Kantō region at 11:58 AM on September 1, 1923, when charcoal cooking fires were lit across Tokyo and Yokohama. The resulting fires, combined with building collapse and tsunami, killed approximately 105,000–142,000 people. Mass panic triggered ethnic massacres of Korean residents by vigilante groups and military, with 6,000–10,000 Koreans killed. The disaster necessitated Japan's largest urban reconstruction until WWII.

events.title: Mukden Incident: Kwantung Army Stages Pretext to Invade Manchuria
events.detail: On September 18, 1931, officers of Japan's Kwantung Army staged a bomb explosion on the South Manchuria Railway near Mukden (modern Shenyang), blamed it on Chinese saboteurs, and used it as a pretext to seize all of Manchuria within weeks. The Japanese government was not consulted — a sign of the military's autonomy under the Meiji Constitution. The League of Nations Lytton Commission condemned Japan; Japan withdrew from the League in 1933. The invasion established Manchukuo as a puppet state.

sources.name: National Archives of Japan — Manchurian Incident

events.title: Second Sino-Japanese War Begins: Marco Polo Bridge Incident
events.detail: On July 7, 1937, a skirmish at the Marco Polo Bridge near Beijing escalated into Japan's full-scale invasion of China. The Imperial Japanese Army captured Shanghai and Nanjing by December 1937. Japan mobilized millions of Chinese civilians as forced labor and drafted Korean and Taiwanese colonial subjects. The war became the costliest conflict in Asian history, with an estimated 8–15 million Chinese civilian deaths by 1945.

sources.name: National Archives of Japan — China Incident

events.title: Nanjing Massacre: Mass Killings in China's Capital
events.detail: Following the capture of Nanjing on December 13, 1937, Japanese forces conducted six weeks of mass killing, rape, and looting known as the Nanjing Massacre or Nanjing Atrocity. The International Military Tribunal for the Far East (Tokyo Trials) documented at least 200,000 deaths. Chinese sources cite 300,000. Westerners in the city — including John Rabe, who organized a 'Safety Zone' — documented the atrocities. The massacre remains the most sensitive issue in Sino-Japanese historical relations.

sources.name: International Military Tribunal for the Far East (Tokyo Trials) Records

events.title: Japan Joins Axis Powers: Tripartite Pact with Germany and Italy
events.detail: On September 27, 1940, Japan signed the Tripartite Pact with Germany and Italy, formalizing the Axis alliance. The pact provided for mutual military assistance if any signatory was attacked by a power not already in the war — specifically aimed at deterring the US from entering either European or Pacific theaters. Japan also concluded a neutrality pact with the Soviet Union in April 1941, securing its northern flank before southward expansion.

events.title: Imperial Navy Attacks Pearl Harbor: US Enters WWII
events.detail: On December 7, 1941 (December 8 in Japan), Japan's carrier strike force launched a surprise attack on the US Pacific Fleet at Pearl Harbor, Hawaii. In two waves, 353 aircraft sank or damaged 19 ships (including 8 battleships), destroyed 188 aircraft, and killed 2,403 Americans. President Roosevelt called it 'a date which will live in infamy' and Congress declared war the next day. The attack made US participation in WWII inevitable, ultimately sealing Japan's defeat.

sources.name: US National Archives — Pearl Harbor Attack Records
National Archives of Japan — Pacific War

events.title: Battle of Midway: Decisive US Victory Turns the Pacific War
events.detail: Between June 4–7, 1942, US forces — aided by codebreaking intelligence (Operation Magic) — ambushed Japan's carrier strike force near Midway Atoll. Four Japanese fleet carriers (Akagi, Kaga, Sōryū, Hiryū) were sunk versus one US carrier (Yorktown). Japan lost irreplaceable experienced naval aviators; the battle ended Japan's strategic initiative in the Pacific. Historians regard Midway as the turning point of the Pacific War.

sources.name: US National Archives — Battle of Midway

events.title: Atomic Bomb 'Little Boy' Dropped on Hiroshima
events.detail: At 8:15 AM on August 6, 1945, the US B-29 Enola Gay dropped the uranium bomb 'Little Boy' on Hiroshima — a city of 350,000 and an important military headquarters. The explosion (equivalent to 12,500 tons of TNT) instantly killed 70,000–80,000 people; by year-end approximately 90,000–140,000 had died from burns, injuries, and acute radiation syndrome. It was the first military use of nuclear weapons in history.

sources.name: Harry S. Truman Library — Atomic Bomb Decision

events.title: Atomic Bomb 'Fat Man' Dropped on Nagasaki; Japan Surrenders
events.detail: On August 9, 1945, the US B-29 Bockscar dropped the plutonium bomb 'Fat Man' on Nagasaki, killing 40,000–80,000 people instantly. The same day, the Soviet Union declared war on Japan and invaded Manchuria. Emperor Hirohito recorded his surrender rescript on August 14; he broadcast it to Japanese citizens on August 15 — the first time most Japanese had heard his voice. Japan formally surrendered aboard USS Missouri on September 2, 1945.

events.title: Allied Occupation Transforms Japan Under MacArthur
events.detail: General Douglas MacArthur arrived in Japan as Supreme Commander, Allied Powers (SCAP) on August 30, 1945. The occupation (1945–1952) implemented sweeping democratic reforms: dismantling the military, breaking up zaibatsu industrial conglomerates, land reform, women's suffrage, new education system, and trade union rights. MacArthur's team drafted a new constitution that Emperor Hirohito promulgated in 1947. The occupation fundamentally reshaped Japanese society.

sources.name: National Archives of Japan — Allied Occupation

events.title: Postwar Constitution Takes Effect: Article 9 Renounces War
events.detail: Japan's current constitution — drafted by SCAP legal staff in early 1946 and enacted May 3, 1947 — introduced popular sovereignty, fundamental human rights, and crucially Article 9: Japan 'forever renounces war as a sovereign right' and 'will never maintain war potential.' The constitution also retained the Emperor as 'symbol of the state' without political power. It has never been formally amended, making it one of the world's most durable constitutions.

sources.name: National Diet Library — Constitution of Japan

events.title: San Francisco Peace Treaty Restores Japanese Sovereignty
events.detail: The Treaty of San Francisco, signed September 8, 1951 (effective April 28, 1952), formally ended the Allied occupation and restored Japan's sovereignty. Japan renounced claims to Korea, Taiwan, the Kurils, and Pacific islands. A simultaneous Japan-US Security Treaty established the US-Japan alliance — American troops stationed in Japan in exchange for a US defense commitment. The treaty framework anchors Japan's foreign policy to the present day.

sources.name: US National Archives — San Francisco Peace Treaty
Japanese Ministry of Foreign Affairs — Treaty Records

events.title: PM Ikeda Launches Income Doubling Plan: The Economic Miracle Begins
events.detail: Prime Minister Hayato Ikeda's National Income Doubling Plan (1960) set a target of doubling real GNP and household incomes within 10 years. Japan exceeded the target in 7 years. GDP grew at 10% annually through the 1960s; Japan hosted the 1964 Tokyo Olympics to showcase its recovery. Japan overtook West Germany to become the world's second-largest economy by 1968. The 'Japanese Economic Miracle' became the 20th century's most remarkable development story.

sources.name: Cabinet Office, Government of Japan — Economic History

events.title: Plaza Accord: Yen Appreciation Reshapes Japan's Economy
events.detail: On September 22, 1985, the G5 nations (US, UK, France, West Germany, Japan) signed the Plaza Accord at New York's Plaza Hotel, agreeing to intervene to depreciate the US dollar. The yen appreciated from 240 to 120 yen per dollar within two years. To offset export losses, the Bank of Japan slashed interest rates — fueling a massive asset price bubble in stocks and real estate that would collapse catastrophically in 1990–91.

sources.name: Bank of Japan — Plaza Accord Historical Review

events.title: Japan's Asset Bubble Peaks: Nikkei at 38,916 and Imperial Palace Land Worth More Than California
events.detail: By December 1989, Japan's Nikkei 225 stock index peaked at 38,916 — up 500% from 1982. Tokyo real estate was so inflated that the Imperial Palace grounds were theoretically worth more than all California real estate combined. Commercial land prices in Tokyo's Ginza district reached $1.5 million per square meter. The Bank of Japan began raising interest rates in 1989, triggering the bubble's collapse in 1990–91.

sources.name: Bank of Japan — Financial Stability Reports

events.title: Asset Bubble Collapses: Japan's 'Lost Decade' Begins
events.detail: Japan's real estate and stock market bubbles collapsed between 1990–1992. The Nikkei fell from 38,916 to below 15,000 by 1992 (an eventual trough below 7,000). Banks held massive non-performing loans; zombie companies were kept alive rather than allowed to fail. The 1990s 'Lost Decade' of stagnation extended into the 2000s — Japan experienced deflation, fiscal deficits, and rising public debt. The experience profoundly influenced global monetary policy thinking.

sources.name: Bank of Japan — Lost Decade Analysis

events.title: Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake Kills 6,434 in Kobe
events.detail: A magnitude 6.9 earthquake struck Kobe at 5:46 AM on January 17, 1995, killing 6,434 people and injuring 43,792. Over 300,000 were left homeless. The earthquake destroyed 100,000 buildings and severed elevated expressways. Japan's emergency response was widely criticized as slow. The disaster spurred legal reforms empowering voluntary disaster response organizations. Kobe was rebuilt within years in one of Japan's largest reconstruction efforts.

events.title: Aum Shinrikyō Sarin Attack on Tokyo Subway
events.detail: On March 20, 1995, members of the Aum Shinrikyō doomsday cult simultaneously released sarin nerve agent on five Tokyo subway lines during rush hour, killing 13 people and injuring 5,800. It was the world's first large-scale chemical terrorism attack in a modern city. Cult leader Shoko Asahara (Chizuo Matsumoto) was arrested, convicted, and executed in 2018. The attack triggered major reforms to Japan's anti-terrorism and religious organization laws.

events.title: 3/11 Triple Disaster: Earthquake, Tsunami, Nuclear Meltdown
events.detail: On March 11, 2011 at 2:46 PM, a magnitude 9.0 megathrust earthquake — Japan's most powerful ever — struck off the Tōhoku coast, triggering a tsunami up to 40 meters high. Approximately 19,750 people died or went missing. The tsunami overwhelmed Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Plant, causing three reactor meltdowns — the worst nuclear accident since Chernobyl. The disaster caused ¥16.9 trillion ($210 billion) in damage and profoundly reshaped Japan's energy policy.

events.title: Abenomics: Three Arrows of Economic Revival Launched
events.detail: Returning to power in December 2012, PM Shinzo Abe launched 'Abenomics' — three arrows of monetary easing (unprecedented BOJ asset purchases), fiscal stimulus, and structural reform. The BOJ targeted 2% inflation and doubled Japan's monetary base. The Nikkei recovered from 8,600 to over 20,000 by 2015. Abenomics partially broke Japan's deflationary mindset but fell short on structural reform. Abe served as PM until 2020 — Japan's longest-serving prime minister.

sources.name: Prime Minister's Office of Japan — Abenomics
IMF — Japan Article IV Abenomics Assessment

events.title: Shinzo Abe Assassinated in Nara — Japan's Longest-Serving PM
events.detail: On July 8, 2022, former Prime Minister Shinzo Abe was shot twice and fatally wounded while delivering a campaign speech outside Yamato-Saidaiji Station in Nara. The attacker, 41-year-old Tetsuya Yamagami, used a homemade firearm. Abe died at Nara Medical University Hospital at 5:03 PM. It was Japan's first assassination of a prominent political figure since 1960. Subsequent investigations revealed Yamagami's grievance against the Unification Church, which had bankrupted his mother.

events.title: Japan Unveils Historic Defense Strategy: Target 2% of GDP by 2027
events.detail: On December 16, 2022, PM Kishida's government adopted three new national security documents — a National Security Strategy, National Defense Strategy, and Defense Buildup Program — the most significant shift in Japan's postwar defense posture. Japan announced plans to raise defense spending to 2% of GDP by FY2027, acquire counterstrike capability (offensive missiles), and nearly double the defense budget from ¥5.4 trillion to ¥8.9 trillion. The strategy cited China, Russia, and North Korea as threats.

sources.name: Japanese Cabinet Secretariat — National Security Strategy 2022
